findings.description: BLS signatures are described and security is asserted ('EUF-CMA under co-CDH in the random oracle model') but no reference is given. The standard citation is Boneh-Lynn-Shacham, 'Short Signatures from the Weil Pairing' (Asiacrypt 2001 / J. Cryptology 17(4):297-319, 2004). Persists from 2026-04-25 run; requires new reference file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a [[BLS01 - Short Signatures from the Weil Pairing|BLS01]] citation and create the corresponding reference file.
sources_consulted: Boneh-Shoup, A Graduate Course in Applied Cryptography v0.6, §15.5 (BLS signatures)

findings.description: Page asserts Schnorr signatures are EUF-CMA secure in the ROM citing only Sch91 (which constructs the scheme) and FS86 (which gives the heuristic). The EUF-CMA proof in the ROM is due to Pointcheval-Stern, 'Security Proofs for Signature Schemes' (Eurocrypt 1996; J. Cryptology 13(3):361-396, 2000) via the forking lemma. Sch91 itself does not contain a ROM EUF-CMA reduction. Persists from 2026-04-25 run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a citation to Pointcheval-Stern 1996/2000 alongside Sch91/FS86.

findings.description: The claim 'Digital signatures imply OWFs' is asserted without citation. The canonical reference is Rompel, 'One-way functions are necessary and sufficient for secure signatures' (STOC 1990), which proves the converse direction (OWF ⇒ DS). The implication DS ⇒ OWF is folklore but the page's argument ('the signing algorithm is a one-way function') is informal and slightly misstates the OWF candidate; cleaner: r ↦ vk where (sk, vk) = KeyGen(r). Persists from 2026-04-25 run.
findings.suggested_fix: Cite Rompel90 and tighten the argument to use r ↦ vk as the OWF candidate.
sources_consulted: Goldreich, Foundations of Cryptography Vol. 2, §6.4 (Rompel's theorem)

findings.description: CLAUDE.md requires `\Pr\!\left[...\right]` for probabilities; both advantage definitions and the correctness statement use plain `\Pr[...]`. Persists from 2026-04-25 run.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `\Pr[...]` with `\Pr\!\left[...\right]` in lines 39, 84, 129. Proposed as a suggested-fix commit in this run's PR.

findings.description: Sign is described as outputting `c` in prose ('$\Sign(\sk, m) \to c,$') but the variable is then immediately renamed to `\sigma`. Persists from 2026-04-25 run.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `\Sign(\sk, m) \to c,` → `\Sign(\sk, m) \to \sigma,`. Proposed as a suggested-fix commit in this run's PR.

findings.description: Section heading says 'Strong Unforgeability' and game macro is `\sufcma` (rendering `suf-cma`) but the prose label says '**SU-CMA unforgeable**' — inconsistent abbreviation. Persists from 2026-04-25 run.
findings.suggested_fix: Use **SUF-CMA unforgeable** (matching the macro and section heading). Proposed as a suggested-fix commit in this run's PR.

findings.description: Prose ends mid-sentence: '...even given many samples' — missing trailing period and the noun is unclear (samples of what?). Persists from 2026-04-25 run.
findings.suggested_fix: Reword to: '...even given oracle access to signatures on adversarially-chosen messages.' Proposed as a suggested-fix commit in this run's PR.

history.note: 8 findings (1 error, 3 missing_citation, 1 notation, 3 nit, 1 error)
Re-review after fix commits e0173d4 (spurious b) and 77f4adf (LS15 rename). 2 error findings RESOLVED. 7 findings remain (3 missing_citation, 1 notation, 3 nit).

findings.description: The page states 'QMA is closed under complement: coQMA = QMA. This uses the quantum error reduction technique (applying the swap test).' This is wrong on two counts. (1) Whether QMA = coQMA is an OPEN problem, analogous to NP vs coNP. The literature treats this as a conditional (e.g., Bookatz 2012 phrases results as 'cannot be in QMA unless QMA = coQMA'), and Mendonça-Hyland-Markham 2021 'Characterising the intersection of QMA and coQMA' explicitly studies QMA ∩ coQMA as a non-trivial subclass. (2) Marriott-Watrous's swap-test technique gives error-reduction (amplification) and closure under intersection, NOT under complement — the swap test compares two purportedly-equal states; it does not flip a verifier's answer.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the claim with: 'QMA is closed under intersection (Marriott-Watrous error reduction via the swap test). Closure under complement — i.e., whether QMA = coQMA — is open, analogous to NP vs coNP.' Proposed as a suggested-fix commit in this run's PR.
sources_consulted: Marriott-Watrous, 'Quantum Arthur-Merlin Games' (Computational Complexity 14(2):122-152, 2005; arXiv:cs/0506068) — amplification + QMA ⊆ PP
Mendonça-Hyland-Markham, 'Characterising the intersection of QMA and coQMA', Quantum Inf. Process. 20(11):396, 2021 (arXiv:2102.03108)

findings.description: Local Hamiltonian QMA-completeness is asserted with explicit 'TODO citation (Kitaev 1999)' marker. Standard reference: Kitaev's original argument appeared in his 1999 lectures and was first published in Kitaev-Shen-Vyalyi, 'Classical and Quantum Computation', AMS Graduate Studies in Mathematics 47, 2002 (§14.4).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'TODO citation (Kitaev 1999)' with a [[KSV02 - Classical and Quantum Computation|KSV02]] wikilink and create the reference file.
sources_consulted: Bookatz, 'QMA-complete problems', arXiv:1212.6312 (survey citing KSV02 as the original Local Hamiltonian QMA-completeness reference)

findings.location: Notable problems / Consistency of local density matrices (line 22)
findings.description: Consistency of local density matrices QMA-completeness is asserted with explicit 'TODO citation' marker. Standard reference: Yi-Kai Liu, 'Consistency of Local Density Matrices is QMA-complete', APPROX-RANDOM 2006 (arXiv:quant-ph/0604166).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'TODO citation' with [[Liu06 - Consistency of Local Density Matrices is QMA-complete|Liu06]] and create the reference file.

findings.description: QMA ⊆ PP is attributed to '(Marriott-Watrous — TODO citation)'. The reference is Marriott-Watrous, 'Quantum Arthur-Merlin Games', Computational Complexity 14(2):122-152, 2005 (arXiv:cs/0506068).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'TODO citation' with [[MW05 - Quantum Arthur-Merlin Games|MW05]] and create the reference file.
sources_consulted: arXiv:cs/0506068 (Marriott-Watrous), abstract states their work yields 'a simple proof that QMA is contained in PP'

findings.description: The page mostly uses `\classQMA`, `\classMA` etc. but inline writes `\mathbf{coQMA}`, `\mathbf{QMA}(2)`, `\mathbf{NEXP}`, `\mathbf{coMA}` directly. macros.ts lacks `\classcoQMA` and `\classNEXP`; either add them or use `\mathbf{...}` for all complexity classes consistently on this page.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `\classcoQMA`, `\classNEXP`, `\classcoMA` macros and use them, or live with the mixed style. (No fix proposed in this run; flagging only.)

findings.description: Page says QMA_IS is 'strictly less powerful than QMA(2), assuming EXP ≠ NEXP'. The actual paper proves QMA_IS ⊆ EXP and QMA_IS(2) = NEXP, giving the strict separation between QMA_IS and QMA_IS(2) (not between QMA_IS and QMA(2)). Both formulations are technically correct under EXP ≠ NEXP since QMA_IS(2) ⊆ QMA(2), but the cleaner / paper-faithful statement is the QMA_IS vs QMA_IS(2) one.
findings.suggested_fix: Optionally restate as 'QMA_IS is strictly less powerful than its two-prover variant QMA_IS(2), assuming EXP ≠ NEXP (the paper shows QMA_IS ⊆ EXP and QMA_IS(2) = NEXP).' (Flagging only.)

history.note: Initial deep review: 6 findings (1 error, 3 missing_citation, 2 nit).

findings.description: Page says 'Classical reductions (without quantum steps) are known for certain parameter regimes — see subsequent work by Peikert' without an explicit citation. The standard references are Peikert, 'Public-key cryptosystems from the worst-case shortest vector problem' (STOC 2009) and Brakerski-Langlois-Peikert-Regev-Stehlé, 'Classical hardness of learning with errors' (STOC 2013). Persists from 2026-04-25 run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add citations [[Pei09 ...|Pei09]] and [[BLPRS13 - Classical hardness of learning with errors|BLPRS13]]; create the corresponding reference files.
sources_consulted: Reg05 reference note (notes that classical reductions came in subsequent work)

findings.description: CLAUDE.md mandates assumption pages follow the order: ## Assumption → ## Known Results → # Variations → # Attacks. This page has: ## Assumption → ## Known Results → # Reduction to lattice problems (top-level, should be ## under Known Results) → ## Cryptographic constructions (orphan ## under a #-level section) → # Attacks → # Variations (Variations and Attacks are reversed). Persists from 2026-04-25 run.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Demote `# Reduction to lattice problems` to `## Reduction to lattice problems` under Known Results. (b) Move `## Cryptographic constructions` somewhere coherent. (c) Put `# Variations` before `# Attacks`.
sources_consulted: CLAUDE.md, Page structure for Assumption pages

findings.description: `### Search–Decision equivalence` is the only ### subsection inside Known Results and reads slightly odd as a single subhead under ##. Acceptable but worth promoting/folding for readability. Persists from 2026-04-25 run.

history.note: Re-review after fix commits 1dff875 (missing comma) and 77f4adf (LS15 rename). 1 error and 1 notation findings RESOLVED. 3 findings remain (1 missing_citation, 1 notation, 1 nit).

findings.description: Wikilink `[[Mer78]]` points to a reference file `content/References/Mer78.md` (or any file with alias `Mer78`) that does not exist. The intended source is Ralph Merkle, "Secure communications over insecure channels", CACM 21(4):294–299, 1978.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `content/References/Mer78 - Secure communications over insecure channels.md` (with alias `Mer78`) and update the wikilink to `[[Mer78 - Secure communications over insecure channels|Mer78]]`. Also update the same broken `[[Mer78]]` wikilink in `content/References/BM09 - Merkle Puzzles Are Optimal An O(n2)-Query Attack on Any Key Exchange from a Random Oracle.md`.
sources_consulted: ls of content/References/ (no Mer78 file present)

findings.description: Page states BM09 "tightened the query complexity of the eavesdropper from $O(\ell^6)$ to the optimal $O(\ell^2)$". IR89's bound was a polynomial; the BM09 reference note says "improves on the $\Omega(n^6)$-query lower bound". The IR89 lower bound on the eavesdropper queries was indeed $O(\ell^6)$ in IR89's analysis, so the page is consistent. No change needed; recording as a passing check.

Page heading style mixes title-case (`# Black-Box Separations`, `## Types of Black-Box Reductions`) with sentence-style elsewhere on the site. Other Glossary pages (`# Algebraic group model`, `# Random oracle model`) use sentence case. Internal consistency is not violated, but cross-page consistency is.
findings.suggested_fix: Decide on a sitewide convention; if sentence-case wins, retitle this page accordingly.

findings.description: `(KA)` is rendered in plain text rather than via a macro. The site does not currently define a `\KA` macro, but the rest of the page uses `\PRF`, `\PRG`, `\OT` — using uppercase plain text for one primitive and macros for others is inconsistent.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a `\KA` macro or wrap occurrences in `\mathsf{KA}` for consistency.
